compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
P0 release-process remediation evaluation (operator-confirmed: NO CHANGES NEEDED).

Concern: 'Releases are insufficiently bound to reviewed and tested source' (v* tag publishes, build-only dependency, mutable action tags, unpinned+unverified mcp-publisher run under OIDC).

This epic's three release stories remediate the P0 comprehensively (21dd/36c7 are unrelated store/config stories):
- Tagged SHA must be a tested, submitted Gerrit main revision -> fd84 (F1): workflow_dispatch-from-main (reviewed by construction) + git merge-base --is-ancestor ancestry check. COVERED.
- Validate vX.Y.Z vs pyproject.toml + server.json -> fd84 (F1): pre-build version-lockstep step asserts version_input == project.version == server.json.version == each packages[].version. COVERED.
- Pin every action to a full SHA -> 08a8 (F4): full-SHA uses:, enforced by real zizmor under make lint. COVERED.
- Pin mcp-publisher + verify checksum/signature -> 08a8 (F4): exact-version pin + sha256sum -c + download->verify->execute split into unprivileged mcp_verify / OIDC-privileged mcp_registry (OIDC never co-resident with an unverified binary) + verify_mcp_publisher_pin.py confirms embedded hash matches the URL. COVERED (beyond the literal ask).
- Build once, preserve provenance, promote exact artifact -> 6168 (F3): build-once -> test exact wheel/sdist in clean jobs -> hash-gated publish (no rebuild) + fixes the Hatchling hook's COMMIT=None wheel-from-sdist defect. COVERED.
- Keep Trusted Publishing + minimal permissions + PEP 740 -> preserved.

DELIBERATE DIVERGENCE (accepted, no change): 'Protect release tags / restrict tag creation to the release identity' is NOT done as written. fd84 instead REMOVES the tag->publish path entirely (publication only via dispatch-from-main gated on the re-keyed required-reviewers pypi environment; a hand-pushed v* tag cannot reach that environment and cannot publish; the tag is a record-only output created after a successful publish). main.tf's no-tag-lock stance stays (no viable GitHub tag-bypass actor in this mirror topology; codified by test_mirror_lock_terraform). The P0's security INTENT (a rogue tag cannot drive an unreviewed publish) is fully met by eliminating the surface rather than gating it. Operator confirmed this design is acceptable; no additional tag-protection layer will be added in this epic.

Operator-attested dependencies remain for fd84 (F1): configure the live pypi environment (required reviewers + main-only branch policy) and the staging-fork TestPyPI E2E; fd84 adds a release-time preflight that queries the environment via REST and fails the release closed if that protection is missing.

compiled_state.description:
# v1.0 release-readiness hardening

Close the five validated readiness findings for `navapbc/rebar` before the v1.0
release, using well-established patterns from actively-maintained OSS projects
(uv, pydantic, pip for release integrity; git repository-format-version for the
store compatibility gate; nginx/OpenSSH for removed-input tombstones). Every
finding was independently verified against current `main` (see per-story
provenance); all five are valid.

## Context

rebar dogfoods a Gerrit-gated `main` (two independent votes: `LLM-Review` +
`Verified`) with GitHub as a read-only mirror, yet the **release and
store-compatibility surfaces sit outside that trust boundary**. The five findings
are independent but share one theme: a control that exists at the code-review
boundary is missing at the release / storage / configuration boundary.

- **Finding 1** — `release.yml` publishes on any human-pushed `v*` tag with no
  machine proof the tagged SHA is the reviewed-and-landed `main`; tags were
  deliberately left unprotected (`infra/terraform-github/main.tf:23-28`).
- **Finding 2** — there is no committed store-format-version / required-capability
  record, so a future release cannot make an already-installed v1.0 binary
  fail-closed on a store it cannot correctly interpret. This gate **must ship in
  v1.0 itself** — a v1.0 binary cannot retroactively learn to inspect a record
  whose semantics did not exist in v1.0. This is the most time-critical story.
  **Legacy-store (absent-record) policy is load-bearing:** every store created by
  a pre-v1.0 build has no record, so the gate must treat *absent* as the implicit
  legacy format (version 0, compatible / fail-**open**) and let the idempotent
  ensure-registry write the record on the next covered write — fail-**closed** only
  when the record is *present but incompatible*. Absent-means-blocked would break
  upgrade-in-place for every existing user; absent-means-silently-bypassed would
  defeat the gate. The expand-contract path and a safe rollback story are part of
  the child (see Finding 2's story).
- **Finding 3** — `python -m build`'s default wheel-from-sdist path bakes
  `COMMIT = None` into the published wheel, and CI installs the source tree
  (`pip install .`) rather than the artifact bundle, so the defect is uncaught
  until after PyPI immutability sets in.
- **Finding 4** — the release pipeline resolves actions, build tools, and a
  privileged OIDC-context binary from mutable references; the repo already
  SHA-pins actions in `gerrit-verify.yaml`, so `release.yml` is inconsistent with
  its own stricter convention.
- **Finding 5** — removed env vars are silently ignored and removed TOML keys get
  only a generic "typo?" warning, including surfaces that redirect the store
  (`TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR`), toggle write gates (`REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE`),
  and change close policy (`verify.require_verdict_for_close`).

**Sequencing (encoded as dependency links).** Finding 2 first (must land in v1.0 to
be effective at all). Findings 1, 3, 4 all edit `release.yml`; they are serialised
by `depends_on` links in the order 4 → 3 → 1 (pin the toolchain, then restructure
build-once, then add the source-authorization gate) — so the Gerrit queue cannot
process them out of order — and file-impact is recorded so `next-batch` also
serialises them. Finding 5 is independent and can proceed in parallel; Finding 2 is
independent (highest priority). `docs/releasing.md` is edited by all three release
stories, so section ownership is assigned per-story (pinning → Finding 4;
build-once → Finding 3; authorization → Finding 1) to avoid overlap.

## Success Criteria

- Releases are initiated from the protected `main` branch via `workflow_dispatch`
  (so the source is reviewed-and-landed by construction — the mainstream OSS
  pattern), the version-lockstep across the version input/pyproject/server.json is
  asserted before build, and publication happens directly via OIDC trusted-publishing
  gated on a required-reviewers `pypi` environment re-keyed to `main`-via-dispatch;
  there is no tag-triggered publish path, so a hand-pushed tag cannot publish.
- A v1.0 binary reads a committed store compatibility record and fails closed
  (reads/diagnostics still allowed) on a **present-but-incompatible** record —
  unknown format version or unrecognised required capability — or a **corrupt/
  unreadable** record, before any side effect across **all** store-mutation/publish
  paths (append/transition/claim/delete/archive, compaction, MCP writes, automatic
  repair, the reconciler inbound write path, and Jira push/reconcile — the last
  gated before the outbound API call, not just the local commit); an **absent**
  record (every pre-v1.0 store) is treated as the legacy format (version 0,
  compatible), and the idempotent ensure-registry writes the record on the next
  covered write, so upgrade-in-place works with no manual step; optional additive
  events keep today's preserve-and-ignore behaviour, and a rollback to a pre-v1.0
  binary leaves the store operable (the record is an ignored-unknown file to old
  binaries).
- The exact wheel and sdist are installed and exercised before publication, the
  bytes tested are the bytes published (hash-verified), and the shipped wheel
  carries the correct non-null source commit.
- Every executable the release depends on is identified by an immutable reference
  (full action SHAs, a hash-locked build environment, an exact-versioned +
  digest-verified MCP publisher) and checkouts do not persist credentials.
- Removed inputs that affect store location, write/sync gates, auth, security, or
  lifecycle policy fail loud with a targeted migration message and a nonzero exit;
  genuinely unknown keys keep the project's chosen forward-compatibility policy.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All five child stories are closed.
- [ ] Each child story's change has landed on `main` through the Gerrit
  two-vote gate (`LLM-Review +1` AND `Verified +1`).
- [ ] The store-compatibility gate (Finding 2) is present in the tree that ships
  as v1.0 (verified by the reader existing and being wired into the write paths),
  since it cannot be added retroactively.
- [ ] The Finding 2 story defines and documents the absent-record (pre-v1.0 store)
  policy — absent = legacy/compatible, record auto-written by the ensure-registry on
  next covered write, fail-closed only when present-but-incompatible — and a
  rollback-safe outcome for a pre-v1.0 binary encountering the record.
- [ ] `docs/releasing.md` and `docs/migrations.md` are updated to describe the new
  release-authorization gate, the build-once artifact flow, and the store
  compatibility record (including the absent-record upgrade/rollback path).
- [ ] Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded with an explicit
  Accept/Reject disposition and a one-line rationale (as a comment on the child
  ticket), and every Accepted finding is reflected in the child's description before
  that child's change receives its Gerrit `LLM-Review +1` / `Verified +1` votes.
compiled_state.title: v1.0 release-readiness hardening
